content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 6, 2004

Bill Schneiderman
Mi-Wuk/Sugar Pine
Fire Protection District
Post Office Box 530
Mi-Wuk Village, California 95346-0530

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-04-228

Dear Mr. Schneiderman:

This letter is in response to your request on behalf of members of the Mi-Wuk/Sugar Pine Fire Protection District for advice on provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ We do not offer advice on past conduct; this letter refers only to actions that have not yet taken place. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(a).) Because your questions are general of nature, we provide you with informal assistance.²

QUESTIONS

1. May the members of the district's board vote on a policy under which the district will purchase supplies from local businesses?

2. May the board members establish a policy permitting volunteer firefighters to post business cards, advertisements, and announcements of local interest on the fire station's bulletin board, which is used by district personnel and members of the local community?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Yes. As we understand the facts, the board members do not have a conflict of interest under the Act in voting on a policy under which the district will purchase supplies from local businesses.

_______________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
² Informal assistance does not confer the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or formal written advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)

File No. 1-04-228
Page No. 2

2. Yes. The Act does not prohibit the board from establishing a policy
regarding use of this bulletin board.

FACTS

The Mi-Wuk/Sugar Pine Fire Protection District is located about 16 miles east of
Sonora, California along the State Highway 108 corridor. The district includes roughly
one square mile of mixed residential and commercial properties, including restaurants, a
gas/mini market and auto parts/repair facility, a village market, real estate offices, etc.
Immediately adjacent to the district is another community that is smaller but provides
some similar services. The firehouse contains a bulletin board which is used by district
personnel and others to post notices of local interest, which can be viewed by district
personnel and visitors to the firehouse.

The district has five members on its board of directors, a full-time fire chief and
assistant fire chief, a part-time secretary, and 16 to 20 volunteer fire fighters. The board
members, the fire chief, and the assistant fire chief file statements of economic interests
(SEIs) under the district's conflict of interest code. The secretary and the volunteer
firefighters do not file SEIs. None of the board members has an economic interest in the
local businesses from which the district might purchase groceries or similar supplies; the
fire chief does or may have an economic interest in the village market. The board may
consider establishing a policy that would govern regular and routine purchases by district
personnel of supplies for the firehouse.

ANALYSIS

Question 1:

A decision on the expenditure of district funds to purchase supplies implicates the
Act's conflict of interest rules, which generally prohibit public officials from making,
participating in, or using their official positions to influence the making of a
governmental decision in which they have a financial interest. The general rule, briefly
stated, is that a conflict of interest exists when a public official plays a decision-making
role in a governmental decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable, material
financial effect on one or more of the official's economic interests. The Commission uses
an eight-step analytical framework to determine whether a public official has a
disqualifying conflict of interest (Regulation 18700(b)(1) – (8), copy enclosed) which we
outline to illustrate the analysis of a decision by the board setting a policy for district
purchases in light of the circumstances you describe.

Step 1: Are the board members public officials?

The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(Sections 87100, 87103; regulation 18701.) "Public Official" is defined by the Act to
include every "member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local by-law
agency," with some exceptions not relevant here. (Section 82048.) "Local government

File No. I-04-228
Page No. 3

agency means a county, city or district of any kind including a school district, or any
other local or regional political subdivision, or any department, division, bureau, office,
board, commission or other agency of the foregoing." (Section 82041.) Members of the
district's board of directors are public officials for purposes of the Act.

Step Two: Will the board members be making, participating in making, or using their
official positions to influence a governmental decision?

The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only where a public official
"make[s], participate[s] in making or in any way attempt[s] to use his [or her] official
position to influence a governmental decision in which he [or she] knows or has reason to
know he [or she] has a financial interest." (Section 87100; regulation 18700(b)(2).) The
Commission has adopted a series of regulations which define "making," "participating in
making," and "influencing" a governmental decision, which provide as follows:

• A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within
the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits
his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on
behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.)

• A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the
authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or intervening
review, the official negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to the decision
maker regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.)

• A public official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or appears before any
member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)³

If a public official does not make, participate in making, or use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision, there can be no conflict of interest as
defined by the Act. Your question presupposes that the board members would be making
or participating in making a governmental decision when they establish a policy for the
purchase of district supplies. A disqualifying conflict of interest is therefore possible.

Step Three: Economic interests, the possible sources of a conflict of interest.

Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:

3 Regulation 18702.4 lists a series of exceptions to the rules stated in the preceding three regulations, which
apply only in certain limited circumstances.

File No. J-04-228
Page No. 4

• A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position of management (section 87103(d); regulation 18703.1(b));

• A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation 18703.2);

• A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3);

• A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the
gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
87103(e); regulation 18703.4);

• A public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family – this is the "personal financial
effects" rule (section 87103; regulation 18703.5).

You have not described the economic interests of the board members, but indicate
that none of them has any economic interest in any individual or business from which the
district might procure the supplies in question. Assuming further that this decision would
not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of the board members'
personal finances, it does not appear that any of the board members has an economic
interest that could be affected by a governmental decision setting district procurement
policies.¹ The conflict analysis therefore ends at this point, with a conclusion that the
board members do not have a conflict of interest in a policy decision setting protocols for
the routine purchase of supplies from local vendors.²

We close by noting that laws outside the Act, such as Government Code section
1090, may also be implicated whenever a government agency enters into a contract with
an agency official, or with any business in which the official has an economic interest.
We cannot provide advice on laws beyond our jurisdiction, but we can advise you to
confer with the Office of the Attorney General about possible applications of

¹Of course, if the board were to adopt a policy that simply conferred discretion in procurement decisions
on a public official who had an economic interest in one of the potential suppliers, it is possible that this
official would have a conflict of interest in exercising the decision-making authority delegated by the
board. For present purposes, we assume that any such discretionary authority would be delegated to a
person who is not a public official, or to a public official who did not have an economic interest in any of
the potential vendors. Our analysis is therefore limited to potential conflicts of interest involving the board
members.

²The next (fourth) step in the analytical process determines whether the officials' established economic
interests are directly or indirectly involved in the decision; the fifth and sixth steps treat the foreseeability
of a material financial effect on the officials' interests. The last two steps presume a finding of reasonably
foreseeable, material financial effect on at least one economic interest, and determine whether the official
may nonetheless vote, under certain circumstances.

File No. I-04-228
Page No. 5

Government Code section 1090, if the district contemplates entering a contract with any
business owned in whole or in part by one of the district's public officials.

Question 2:

As public officials acting in their official capacities, the board members would be
making a governmental decision if they were to establish a policy regarding use of the
firehouse bulletin board. You have not, however, suggested that such a decision would
have a reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on the economic interest of any
board member. So long as that is the case, the Act's conflict of interest provisions would
not bar any board member from voting on such a policy. Nor is there anything in the Act
that would prohibit the district's volunteer firefighters from posting their business cards
or similar commercial notices, or announcements of upcoming events that may be of
interest to district personnel or visiting members of the public, on this bulletin board.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosures

district will purchase supplies from local businesses?
2. May the board members establish a policy pennitting volunteer firefighters to
post business cards, advertisements, and unrrouoceroents of local interest on
the fire station's bulletin board, which is used by district personnel and
members ofthe local communit5fl
2. Yes. The Act does not prohibit the board from establishing a policy
regarding use of this bulletin board.

CONCLUSION: I ' Yes. As we understand the facts, the board members do not have a conflict
of interest under the Act in voting on a policy under which the district will
purchase supplies from local businesses.
